title: Journalling tags and pocket labels
subtitle: Cutting, punching, and writing tags for page decoration and pocket inserts
excerpt: Tags and labels add a tactile layer to a journal or scrapbook: a small card hanging from a reinforced hole, a pocket insert folded into a page pocket, or a paper strip labelling a section of the journal. The technique is basic, cut, punch, write, attach, but the design choices (size, shape, colour, lettering style) give each journal its character.
sourceNotes: Paper labels and tags are a continuous part of document and archive practice from at least the eighteenth century. Victorian-era scrapbook and album manuals describe tab and label making as a standard page-organisation technique.

glossaryTerms.definition: A small card or label with a punched hole, attached to a journal page by string, ribbon, or a brad fastener. Tags carry short annotations, dates, quotations, or decorative elements.

A small metal ring pressed through a punched hole to reinforce it, preventing tearing. Eyelets are set with a hole punch and a setting tool. In journalling, they provide a clean, permanent hole for hanging tags.

A folded card or paper tucked into a page pocket. Pocket inserts can carry notes, photographs, receipts, or folded sub-pages. The pocket is usually made from a half-page folded at the bottom and attached to the page at its sides.

content.text: work because they separate information from the main page surface. A date, a note, a quotation, on the page they compete with everything else; hung from a punched corner they float above the page and draw the eye without interrupting the main content. The technique is cheap and quick; the value is in how the tag is designed and lettered.

items.name: Cartridge paper, 130 gsm, or off-cuts of coloured card
items.substitutions: Endpaper stock for a richer surface; Kraft paper for a postal/archive look

items.name: Washi tape, for reinforcing hole edges and decorating
items.qty: one or two rolls in coordinating colours

content.text: Cut a rectangle of card to the desired size, 50 × 90 mm is a standard luggage-tag size that leaves enough room for lettering.
Cut a notch or taper at one short end to give the tag its shape. A simple angled cut at the two corners produces a traditional tag outline.
Apply a small piece of washi tape over the point where the hole will be punched, on both sides of the tag, to reinforce the area before punching. Press firmly and smooth with a fingernail.
Punch the hole centred on the reinforced tab area, approximately 8 mm from the top edge.
Letter the tag before attaching, it is easier to write on a flat surface. Thread with twine, ribbon, or a small elastic ring through the punched hole.

To make a page pocket: cut a card slightly narrower than the journal page width. Fold up the bottom third. Apply washi tape or PVA to the two side edges of the folded bottom section and press to the page surface. The open top creates the pocket.
can be set in the pocket top edge to allow a tag to hang from the pocket's corner.

items.symptom: The tag tears at the hole after a few uses
items.cause: Hole was punched too close to the edge; paper not reinforced.
items.fix: Reinforce with washi tape before punching. Position the hole at least 8 mm from the nearest edge.

items.symptom: The pocket pulls away from the page
items.cause: Side edges were not adhered fully, or the adhesive used was too weak.
items.fix: Run washi tape the full height of both side edges rather than just a small patch. Press firmly and allow to set before inserting anything into the pocket.
